channel.title: healthTest
channel.description: This is a randomly ordered feed of the most recent entries from healthTest, skipping any feeds with errors.

item.title: The Scan by 2 Minute Medicine®: Daily caffeine intake linked to lower dementia risk, AAP sues over federal vaccine schedule overhaul, Catherine O’Hara passes away after short illness, and GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce endometrial cancer risk
item.description: The Scan by 2 Minute Medicine® is a pop-culture medical newsletter and exclusive benefit for 2 Minute Medicine Plus subscribers. Moderate caffeine int...

item.title: Polypharmacy is associated with higher risk of mortality and hospitalizations among older adults
item.description: 1. Polypharmacy was associated with a higher risk of mortality and hospitalization at one-year and five-years among older adults aged >75 years. Evide...

item.title: How Biologics Continue to Transform Dermatologic Care
item.description: Despite some remaining challenges, biologic therapy continues to revolutionize the care of people with psoriasis, atopic dermatitis, and many other co...

item.title: Step Therapy Continues to Bedevil IBD Treatment
item.description: Young adults with IBD are more likely to face step therapy mandates, making clinicians concerned that insurers are ignoring evidence-based guidelines ...

item.title: Rewards vs. goals: How a brain signal used to study depression tells us about our immediate desires
item.description: James Cavanagh has been at the University of New Mexico for 13 years studying cognitive neuroscience and using imaging tools to understand psychiatric...

item.title: Reassuring Data on C-Section Infection Control
item.description: Adjunctive azithromycin had no significant impact on childhood obesity or other outcomes. Medscape Medical News

item.title: New AI tool helps scientists see how cells work together inside diseased tissue
item.description: Doctors and scientists have long relied on microscopes to study human tissue and diagnose disease. But today's medical research produces far more info...

item.title: More social support can sometimes lead to more—and sometimes to less—use of mental health care
item.description: People with severe mental health problems are generally thought to receive more care when they are unwell, and less care once they begin to recover. A...

item.title: Doctors discover a simple method to predict the risk of brain tumor recurrence
item.description: Meningioma is the most common type of brain tumor. It does not develop in the brain tissue itself, but on the inside of the meninges, the membranes th...

item.title: Molecular Markers May Predict Nut Allergy Challenge Failure
item.description: In children with tree nut or peanut allergy, molecular markers identified by component-resolved diagnostics may predict oral food challenge failure. M...

item.title: Q&A: What About Biosimilars in Dermatology?
item.description: The biggest misconception regarding biosimilars is that they are so different from the originator that you cannot trust them, says Steven Feldman, MD,...

item.title: Why Medicare must cover atrial fibrillation screening to prevent strokes
item.description: Evelyn felt fine that morning. At 68, she was active, healthy, and just returned from her daily walk when her heart began to flutter. It wasn’t painfu...

item.title: Teaching joy transforms the future of medical practice [PODCAST]
item.description: Subscribe to The Podcast by KevinMD. Watch on YouTube. Catch up on old episodes! Psychiatrist Sarah Hollander, clinical psychologist Kelly D. Holder, ...

item.title: Digital twin reveals how eye cells lose their organization in leading cause of vision loss
item.description: National Institutes of Health (NIH) researchers have developed a digital replica of crucial eye cells, providing a new tool for studying how the cells...

item.title: Validating AI in health care: the role of real-world evidence
item.description: Before complex medical interventions ever reach patients, their effectiveness must be scientifically proven through rigorous testing in controlled set...

item.title: In a world built for sitting, here's how to stay active — even when stuck inside
item.description: In the office, classroom and living room, working and relaxing mean sitting still. Our bodies evolved without chairs. Here are some tips for getting o...

item.title: Hospitals are posting prices for patients. It's mostly industry using the data
item.description: The Trump administration pushed for price transparency in health care. But instead of patients shopping for services, it's mostly health systems and i...

item.title: This complex brain network may explain many of Parkinson's stranger symptoms
item.description: Parkinson's disease appears to disrupt a brain network involved in everything from movement to memory.

item.title: The incidence of psychotic disorders has increased in more recent birth cohorts
item.description: 1. The incidence of psychotic disorders in Ontario has increased in more recent birth cohorts, and the age of diagnosis has decreased. Evidence Rating...

item.title: Status epilepticus cause may predict likelihood of neurologic recovery but not short-term mortality
item.description: 1. The underlying cause of status epilepticus is strongly associated with the likelihood of neurologic recovery, but does not independently predict sh...

item.title: Frailty and functional decline: Why diagnosis is not enough
item.description: As clinicians, we are trained to look for disease. We measure blood pressure, blood sugar, lung function, and imaging results, often waiting for patho...

item.title: Why Filipino nurses faced higher COVID-19 mortality rates

item.title: Oral nonstatin medication significantly lowers cholesterol in high-risk patients
item.description: 1. In this randomized controlled trial, once-daily oral enlicitide was associated with sustained reductions in low-density lipoprotein cholesterol lev...

item.title: Modest 'mental exercise' can reduce risk of dementia for decades, study finds
item.description: One type of cognitive training appears to reduce the risk of dementia 20 years later.

item.title: Olympic COVID restrictions are gone, but some athletes are still self-quarantining
item.description: For most people, the pandemic days of masking are behind them. In certain corners of the Winter Olympics, though, things still look a lot like they di...
